title: The Problem-Preservation Trap

content.statement: Institutions quietly preserve the very problem they were built to solve.
content.explainer: When your income depends on a problem existing, actually solving it ends the paycheck, so organizations drift toward managing problems forever instead of ending them. The stronger the earnings from the problem, the more the incentive bends against the cure.
questions: Which of your recurring revenues depends on a customer problem never fully going away?
If you solved your clients' problem permanently tomorrow, how much of your income disappears with it?
Where does your team have a quiet incentive to manage a problem forever rather than end it?
sop: Name a problem your business or a department is paid to address.
Add up the recurring revenue that flows only while that problem persists.
Add up what you'd earn from solving it permanently, often a one-time or shrinking sum.
Divide problem revenue by solving revenue to expose your true preservation incentive (see calculation, page 6).
Redesign pricing or incentives so you profit more from resolution than from the problem's survival.

definitions.def: Any established organization or system, a company, agency, charity, or department, that develops its own interest in continuing to exist.
The observation that institutions will try to preserve the problem to which they are the solution, because ending the problem would end their reason to exist.

content.equation: Preservation Risk = Revenue from the Problem ÷ Revenue from Solving It
content.equation_example: Revenue while the problem persists: $900k/yr. Revenue from actually solving it: $100k one-time. $900k ÷ $100k = 9. A ratio far above 1 means you earn far more from the problem than the cure, so expect it to be managed forever; near or below 1 means solving it pays.
content.closing_statement: Watch who profits from a wound staying open, because they are rarely the ones who will close it.
content.question: Which of your recurring revenues depends on a customer problem never fully going away?

caption:
Principle Nº 339: The Problem-Preservation Trap

Institutions quietly preserve the very problem they were built to solve.

Ask yourself: Which of your recurring revenues depends on a customer problem never fully going away?
